q: A capacitor is used as an electric circuit component to store electrical energy.
The capacitor is dissimilar to a rechargeable battery.
The capacitor was originally known as a condenser.
A capacitor consists of two insulators separated by a small distance.
An insulator is inserted between its conductors.
Film capacitors are the ones that use ceramic as the dielectric medium.
Film capacitors are available in nearly any value and working voltages up to $1500\text{ V}$.
Ceramic capacitors are mainly used for low stability performance.
In ceramic capacitors, one can develop both high capacitance and low capacitance by altering the thickness of the ceramic disc.
The capacitance values of ceramic capacitors are unstable with respect to the applied voltage, frequency and temperature.
Electrolytic capacitors are the ones that use the oxide layer as the dielectric material.
Tantalum and aluminum are the mainly two types of ceramic capacitors.
The failure of electrolytic capacitors can be hazardous, resulting in an explosion or fire.
Variable capacitors mostly use air as the dielectric medium.
The capacitance of variable capacitors cannot be mechanically adjusted.
A supercapacitor (SC) is a high-capacity capacitor with a capacitance value much lower than other capacitors.
Supercapacitors typically store $10$ to $100$ times more energy than electrolytic capacitors.
Supercapacitors accept and deliver charge much slower than batteries.
Film capacitors and ceramic capacitors are polarized capacitors.
Capacitors are used for blocking AC current after getting fully charged.
Capacitors are used for blocking DC current after getting fully charged but allow the AC current to pass through it.
Capacitors can be used as the sensor for measuring humidity, fuel levels, mechanical strain, etc.
When a capacitor is connected to a battery the two conductors of the capacitor have charges of unequal magnitude.
The magnitude of the charge on each conductor, $Q$, is called the charge of the capacitor.
The quantity of charge $Q$ on the capacitor is inversely proportional to the potential difference $V$ between the conductors.
The capacitance of a capacitor is defined as the ratio of the charge to the potential difference of the capacitor.
In the SI system, the unit of capacitance is coulomb.
$1\text{ F}$ is equivalent to $1\text{ C V}^{-1}$.
The unit farad is named in honor of Michael Faraday.
In practice, typical devices have capacitances ranging from microfarad ($\mu\text{F}$) to picofarad ($\text{pF}$).
When the charge on the capacitor is increased, the potential difference decreases proportionally.
The capacitance of a capacitor depends on the size and the shape of the capacitor.
The capacitance of a capacitor does not depend on the nature of the insulator between the two conductors.
The amount of charge given to a conductor to change its potential by one unit is called the electric capacity of the conductor.
The SI unit of electric capacity is also the volt.
A parallel-plate capacitor is the simplest capacitor.
Charging a capacitor means connecting the capacitor to a resistor.
The surface charge density on each plate is $\sigma = \frac{Q}{A}$.
If the plates are very close together, the electric field between the plates is non-uniform.
The value of the electric field between the plates can be expressed in terms of surface charge density $\sigma$ as $E = \frac{\sigma}{\epsilon}$.
The magnitude of the potential difference $V$ between the plates equals $E d$.
The capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor is directly proportional to the separation of the plates.
For a given insulating material the capacitance of a parallel-plate capacitor is directly proportional to the area of its plates.
The permittivity of vacuum $\epsilon_0$ is $8.85 \times 10^{-12}\text{ C}^2 \text{ N}^{-1} \text{ m}^{-2}$.
For a vacuum, the dielectric constant $\kappa$ is equal to zero.
The dielectric constant of water at $18^\circ\text{C}$ is $81$.
The dielectric constant of an insulating material is the ratio of its permittivity to that of a vacuum.
If there is a vacuum between its plates, its capacitance $C_0$ is $C_0 = \frac{\epsilon_0 d}{A}$.
The ratio of the capacitance of a capacitor with an insulating material to that with a vacuum is called the dielectric constant.
When a charged capacitor is disconnected from a battery and the air medium between its plates is replaced by an insulating material, the potential difference is found to increase.
When an insulating material is inserted into a disconnected charged capacitor, the charge $Q$ of the capacitor does not change.
Insulating materials have values of dielectric constant $\kappa$ less than unity.
A dielectric provides an increase in capacitance.
A dielectric provides possible mechanical support between the plates, which allows the plates to be close together without electrical contact.
Film capacitors range from $10\%$ to $0.01\%$ in tolerance.
Electrolytic capacitors are used for energy storage in flash devices.
Smaller units of supercapacitors are used as power backup for static random-access memory (SRAM).
Capacitors can also be used in a time-dependent circuit (timer circuit).
The relationship between charge, capacitance, and potential difference can be written as $Q = CV$.
The ratio of potential differences $\frac{V_0}{V}$ is equal to the dielectric constant $\kappa$.
A dielectric increases the maximum operating voltage of a capacitor.
The physical size of a capacitor changes when a dielectric is inserted.
The dielectric may be able to withstand a larger electric field between the plates than air.
The permittivity of free space $\varepsilon_0$ is equal to $8.85 \times 10^{-12}\text{ C}^2\text{ N}^{-1}\text{ m}^{-2}$.
When the distance between the two parallel plates of a capacitor is doubled, its capacitance increases.
When the distance between two parallel plates having charges of equal magnitude and opposite signs is reduced, the capacitance decreases.
When an insulating material is inserted between the conductors of a capacitor, its capacitance increases.
A capacitor stores electrical energy in the form of a magnetic field.
A capacitor stores electrical energy in the form of an electric field.
When a capacitor is charged, charge is transferred from a conductor at higher potential to a conductor at lower potential.
Work has been done for the transfer of charge from a lower potential conductor to a higher potential conductor.
During the charging process, the magnitude of the charge on the two conductors increases gradually.
The potential difference between the conductors decreases gradually as the capacitor charges.
The energy stored by the capacitor can be analyzed from the $Q-V$ graph.
The work done in charging the capacitor is given by the shaded area under the curve of a $Q-V$ graph.
The area under the $Q-V$ curve that represents work done is a shaded rectangle.
The work done in charging the capacitor is equal to $\frac{1}{2}QV$.
Before a capacitor is charged, each of its conductors already carries a full maximum charge.
Before a capacitor is charged, the potential difference between the two conductors is zero.
The average potential difference of the capacitor before and after it is charged is $\frac{V}{2}$.
The work done $W$ for transferring a charge of $Q$ between two conductors is given by $W = \bar{V}Q$.
The electrical energy stored by the capacitor is kept in the form of a gravitational field.
Since $C = Q/V$, the energy $W$ of a capacitor can be expressed as $W = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$.
The energy $W$ of a capacitor can be expressed as $W = \frac{1}{2}\frac{Q^2}{C}$.
Placing a resistor in the charging circuit speeds up the charging process.
Placing a resistor in the charging circuit slows down the charging process.
The greater the values of resistance and capacitance, the longer it takes to charge the capacitor.
The greater the values of resistance and capacitance, the shorter it takes to charge the capacitor.
During charging, the capacitor voltage rises with time according to the equation $V_c = V_0(1 - e^{-t/RC})$.
The product of the resistance $R$ and the capacitance $C$ is called the time constant $\tau$.
The time constant $\tau$ of a capacitor charging or discharging process is measured in seconds.
The time constant $\tau$ of a capacitor charging or discharging process is measured in Farads.
After being charged, a capacitor can be discharged through a load resistor $R_L$.
During discharging, the voltage of the capacitor falls off with time according to the equation $V_c = V_0(e^{-t/R_L C})$.
Several capacitors can be connected together to be used in a variety of applications.
There are two types of combinations of capacitors: parallel and series.
There are three types of combinations of capacitors: parallel, series, and perpendicular.
All capacitors connected in parallel have the same potential difference.
Capacitors connected in parallel always carry identical amounts of charges even if their capacitances differ.
If $Q$ is the total charge on three parallel capacitors, then $Q = Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3$.
An equivalent capacitor has the same total charge $Q$ when the same potential difference $V$ is applied.
The equivalent capacitance of capacitors connected in parallel is the sum of the capacitances of the individual capacitors.
The equivalent capacitance $C_p$ of a parallel combination of $n$ identical capacitors is $C_p = nC$.
The equivalent capacitance $C_p$ of a parallel combination of $n$ identical capacitors is $C_p = \frac{C}{n}$.
When capacitors are connected in series, each capacitor has the same charge.
When capacitors are connected in series, each capacitor has the same potential difference.
Capacitors connected in series have different potential differences if their capacitances are different.
The total potential difference $V$ of a series combination of three capacitors is $V = V_1 + V_2 + V_3$.
When capacitors are connected in series, the reciprocal of the equivalent capacitance is the sum of the reciprocals of their individual capacitances.
The equivalent capacitance $C_s$ of a series combination of $n$ identical capacitors is $C_s = \frac{C}{n}$.
In a parallel connection of capacitors, each capacitor has the same charge.
In a series connection of capacitors, each capacitor has the same charge.
In a parallel connection of capacitors, the potential difference of each capacitor is the same.
A parallel-plate capacitor is the simplest capacitor.
A parallel-plate capacitor consists of two parallel metal plates separated by air or other insulating material.
The ratio of the capacitance with an insulating material to the capacitance with a vacuum is called the dielectric constant.
If one conductor of a capacitor has a charge $+Q$, the other has a charge $-Q$.
The charge of a capacitor is the sum of the magnitudes of the charges on both conductors combined.
The potential difference of a capacitor is twice the potential difference between the two conductors of the capacitor.
Relative permittivity is listed as one of the key words on page 144 of the textbook.
